turns.content: I'm back in the game after five years and trying to fix a race condition in our production service. It seems like I just need to use AtomicUsize everywhere instead of Mutex, so why is that not working for my counter?

AtomicUsize is great for simple counters but it doesn't automatically guarantee visibility across threads unless you specify memory ordering. If your compiler reorders instructions or caches values locally, the counter won't update correctly even though the atomic type itself handles single reads and writes safely.
Okay so I should add SeqCst to every AtomicUsize access? That sounds like a lot of boilerplate for just incrementing a number.

Exactly, but you only need SeqCst if your code depends on memory ordering guarantees beyond the single operation. For simple counters where you just read and write without interleaving complex logic with other threads, Relaxed is usually sufficient because it's fast and the CPU handles most of the synchronization for you.
What about AtomicBool? I tried using that to track whether a feature flag was enabled but it keeps showing stale values in some threads. Is there something special about how bools are handled?

AtomicBool has the same memory ordering requirements as AtomicUsize, so if you're seeing stale reads, your thread is likely relying on Relaxed ordering when it needs stronger guarantees. You need to explicitly set the ordering when loading or storing the boolean value.
I don't want to overcomplicate this with all these orderings if I can avoid them. Is there a pattern where I can just use AtomicPtr and let it handle everything?

AtomicPtr is useful for sharing ownership of data without copying, but it doesn't magically solve memory ordering issues either. If you're using AtomicPtr to point into a shared struct, the pointer itself might be ordered correctly but the struct contents could still be out of sync depending on your access pattern.
This is getting confusing. What's the actual difference between SpinLock and AtomicUsize? Can I just replace my Mutex with a simple atomic counter?

A SpinLock uses an atomic variable internally to prevent busy-waiting loops, typically an AtomicBool or AtomicUsize that tracks whether the lock is held. But unlike Mutex which blocks other threads via a queue, Atomic-based locking just spins until it can acquire the lock without blocking the thread.
So if I build my own spinlock using AtomicUsize with Relaxed ordering, will that actually work for protecting shared state in production?

You can use it but you must be careful about the memory ordering. For a simple boolean flag indicating lock ownership, AtomicBool with Acquire-Release is ideal because it ensures writes are visible to readers while preventing unnecessary reordering of unrelated operations.
Wait so I need different orderings for different parts of my code? Like Acquire when reading and Release when writing?

Yes, that's the key insight. When you write to an atomic variable, use Release ordering to ensure all prior writes become visible to other threads before your write completes. When you read, use Acquire to ensure any subsequent reads see only after the release has completed.
I'm getting weird race conditions in our producer-consumer queue implementation and I think the issue is how we're managing shared state without proper synchronization.

That sounds like a classic case of forgetting to use atomic types for shared variables. Instead of using regular integers or pointers that can be interleaved, you should consider using AtomicUsize for counters and AtomicPtr for the queue head/tail. The real problem is usually memory ordering; if you just use Relaxed without understanding how it affects visibility across cores, your checks might see stale values.
I've tried using AtomicUsize but my read operations sometimes return the old value even after the write happened. It feels like the compiler is reordering things in a way I didn't expect.

You're seeing this because Relaxed ordering doesn't guarantee that reads see writes that occurred before them unless you also acquire those specific loads. Think of it like two people writing on separate whiteboards in different rooms; without a signal light (memory fence), they can't be sure when the other person's board is updated relative to their own. You need Acquire ordering for reads and Release for writes to ensure the memory barrier happens.
Okay so I should use Acquire on my consumer side and Release on the producer side? But what about when I'm just checking a flag?

For simple flags like shutdown signals, AtomicBool works well. If you only need to check if something is true without reading other data, Relaxed might suffice since there's no dependency chain, but for anything that affects the queue state or counters, stick with Acquire/Release pairs. The key intuition is that atomic operations are more than just single-word locks; they're about establishing a causal relationship between threads.
I'm still stuck on building the spinlock part of this. I want to avoid using std::sync but implement it with atomics myself for learning purposes.

You can build a lock by combining an AtomicBool (to track whether someone holds the lock) and an AtomicPtr or AtomicUsize (for the counter). The tricky part is ensuring that when you release, all previous writes are visible before the flag flips. Think of it like a turnstile: the first person must ensure everyone behind them has seen their entry signal before letting anyone else through.
So I need to make sure the release happens after the counter is updated, otherwise another thread might see an old state and think it's safe to proceed.

Exactly. That's why SeqCst ordering is often used for spinlocks because it combines all memory orderings into one, making correctness easier to reason about even if you don't track each operation individually. For your production queue, I'd recommend using AtomicUsize for the counter with Release on decrement and Acquire on increment, plus an AtomicBool for the busy flag.

I have a counter in my Rust tooling that I'm incrementing from multiple threads using simple integers, but the values keep getting corrupted. I tried wrapping it in an AtomicUsize and just calling load() without any special flags, assuming that would be enough to fix the race condition.

That's a good start since you are using AtomicUsize which is designed for this. However, when you call load() and store() on an AtomicUsize without specifying memory ordering, it defaults to SeqCst (Sequential Consistency). This means it ensures all memory operations before the load/store are globally visible in the same order across threads, but it also adds overhead that might be unnecessary if your threads don't strictly need that strict global ordering.
Right, so I thought Relaxed was fine because I just want to read and write the value. Does Relaxed guarantee that the load will actually see a consistent state without any barriers?

Relaxed does not guarantee visibility of operations across threads by itself, but it guarantees that the operation on the atomic variable is visible to other threads eventually. The catch is you need to combine Relaxed with Acquire or Release semantics on your neighboring operations if those operations aren't atomics themselves.
Wait, so if I'm just doing a simple counter update in isolation, maybe I can get away with SeqCst even though it's slower?

In many practical cases for counters where you only read and write the same variable without complex dependencies between different variables, SeqCst is perfectly adequate because it prevents reordering of your specific counter updates. But if you later need to synchronize with another thread that's doing work on a different variable using Relaxed ordering, the lack of barriers becomes problematic.
That makes sense. So for my current spinlock implementation where I'm just checking and setting flags, maybe I can use AtomicBool with SeqCst?

Using AtomicBool is fine for the flag itself since it's a single boolean value, but remember that your actual lock mechanism usually involves checking if the flag is false while holding some other state. If you're only using the atomic as a simple toggle without coordinating with other memory operations in your spinlock logic, then yes, AtomicBool works well.
But what about when I need to implement a proper mutex where threads actually wait? Is there a pattern for that?

For a proper mutex you typically use AtomicPtr<Mutex><'static> instead of just an atomic flag. You store the pointer to your actual Mutex implementation in an AtomicPtr, and when acquiring the lock you check if the pointer is null using Acquire ordering to ensure you see any writes that happened before setting it.
So I'm confused about whether my current approach of just swapping integers in a loop is actually building a real lock or just busy waiting?

You are currently doing spin-waiting with an atomic flag, but that's not the same as a proper mutex. A proper mutex uses synchronization primitives like a condition variable to block threads instead of spinning in a loop. Your current approach wastes CPU cycles because you're checking the flag repeatedly without giving up the CPU.
So I should stop using AtomicUsize for this counter and switch to something else?

You don't necessarily need to change the data type, but you do need to reconsider your synchronization strategy. If you just want a simple thread-safe counter without complex locking logic, AtomicUsize with Relaxed ordering combined with appropriate Acquire/Release barriers is often sufficient and more efficient than SeqCst for high-frequency updates.
Can you clarify the difference between using a simple atomic flag versus building a proper mutex with AtomicPtr?

A simple atomic flag is just a boolean that tells you whether someone else has taken some action, but it doesn't provide mutual exclusion itself. A proper mutex using AtomicPtr<Mutex><'static> actually implements the lock mechanism by storing the state of the actual Mutex object and ensuring threads can safely access it without data races.
I think I understand now. So my initial assumption that any atomic type would solve all my threading problems was wrong?

Exactly, and that's a common mistake when moving to Rust concurrency. The specific atomic type you choose matters less than the memory ordering you pair it with and whether your logic actually requires mutual exclusion or just safe data access.

Why does this even work this way? I tried to write a counter in my game server but the documentation makes it sound like magic. Why do we need these fancy atomic wrappers instead of just regular integers?

That's a great question. Regular integers don't guarantee visibility across threads because the compiler and CPU might optimize away reads or writes if they believe the value never changes. Atomic types like AtomicUsize, AtomicBool, and AtomicPtr prevent this by using special hardware instructions to ensure every read is immediately visible to all other cores.
But I still don't get it. It sounds like just a variable with a fancy name. Why can't I just use std::sync::Mutex and forget about this? I'm trying to build a simple counter for my proof of concept.

You're right that Mutex works, but it involves acquiring locks which adds overhead. For high-frequency updates in your game server, atomic operations are lighter because they don't block threads with a lock acquisition cycle. Think of it like this: a mutex is like a single-lane bridge where only one car passes at a time, while atomics are like a wide highway lane where cars can pass through each other if their timing aligns perfectly.
I'm still confused about the memory ordering part. Why do we have Relaxed, Acquire, Release, and SeqCst? Can't I just pick one and never change it?

Memory ordering controls how the CPU arranges memory operations relative to each other. Relaxed is the weakest guarantee - it only ensures the atomic operation itself happens but doesn't enforce any ordering of other operations. Acquire and Release are stronger: Acquire makes sure all previous writes become visible before this read, while Release ensures this write becomes visible to subsequent reads in other threads.
This is getting complicated. I just want my counter to work reliably without thinking about all these ordering rules. Is there a default that's safe?

For most counting scenarios in your game server, SeqCst (sequential consistency) is actually the safest default because it enforces a global order of operations across all threads. However, if you only need to know whether a flag has changed, AtomicBool with Acquire or Release might suffice since you're not reading intermediate values between complex operations.
I'm still frustrated because I feel like I have to manually manage all these ordering guarantees. Why doesn't the compiler just handle this automatically?

The Rust compiler can infer some orderings, but explicit memory ordering is crucial for performance-critical code in your game server because incorrect assumptions can lead to subtle bugs that only appear under heavy load. Think of it like building a bridge: you need specific materials and engineering standards to ensure safety under stress. The compiler doesn't know the full context of your program's requirements.
Okay, I understand the theory better now but how do I actually implement this for my counter? Can you show me a basic example without too many complications?

You can use AtomicUsize with SeqCst ordering like this: let counter = std::sync::atomic::AtomicUsize::new(0); then increment it with counter.fetch_add(1, Ordering::SeqCst). This ensures the update is immediately visible to other threads while maintaining the correct memory order.
What if I need more complex synchronization like a spinlock? I've heard you can build those from atomics but I don't understand how exactly.

A simple spinlock uses an AtomicBool or AtomicUsize to track a boolean state. You loop checking the atomic value with Acquire ordering until it's false, then perform your critical section, and finally set it back to true using Release ordering so other threads know they can proceed.
This feels fragile though. What if my loop never terminates? Is there a way to make this more robust for production code?

You should wrap the spinloop in a try block and handle the timeout explicitly, or better yet, use a proper Mutex when you need guaranteed termination. For your proof of concept though, understanding these building blocks will help you decide when to stick with atomics versus switching to higher-level synchronization primitives.
I think I'm getting somewhere but the trade-offs are still unclear. When exactly should I reach for atomic operations instead of mutexes in my actual game server code?

Use atomics when you have very frequent small updates like counters or flags where lock contention would be too high, and when the operations are simple enough that you can reason about their memory ordering. For complex data structures or longer critical sections, Mutex is usually safer despite the overhead.
